solar-system.question: Which planet is third from the Sun?
solar-system.explanation: Order from the Sun: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars…

solar-system.question: What is the Sun mainly composed of?
solar-system.explanation: The Sun is a star powered by fusion of light elements.

solar-system.question: What is an astronomical unit (AU) often used to mean?
solar-system.explanation: 1 AU is a convenient Earth-centered distance scale in the solar system.

solar-system.question: What is the asteroid belt mostly between?
solar-system.explanation: Many small rocky bodies orbit between Mars and Jupiter.

solar-system.question: What is a comet’s icy tail generally pushed by?
solar-system.explanation: Comet tails point away from the Sun as material is blown outward.

solar-system.question: Why do planets stay in orbit around the Sun?
solar-system.correctAnswer: Gravity provides the centripetal force for curved paths
incorrectAnswers: Planets are weightless so no force
solar-system.explanation: Newtonian gravity explains closed orbits when speed and distance balance.

solar-system.question: Which planet has the largest mass in the solar system?
solar-system.explanation: Jupiter is the most massive planet.

solar-system.question: What is Pluto classified as today (common category)?
solar-system.explanation: Pluto is a dwarf planet in the Kuiper belt region.

solar-system.question: What is the Kuiper belt?
solar-system.correctAnswer: A region of icy bodies beyond Neptune
solar-system.explanation: Many trans-Neptunian objects reside in the Kuiper belt.

solar-system.question: What is the Oort cloud (conceptual)?
solar-system.correctAnswer: A distant spherical reservoir of cometary bodies
solar-system.explanation: Long-period comets are often linked to distant icy reservoirs.

solar-system.question: Why does Venus appear very bright from Earth?
solar-system.correctAnswer: It is close to Earth and its clouds reflect lots of sunlight
incorrectAnswers: It emits its own light like a star
It is always behind the Sun
solar-system.explanation: Venus’s thick clouds make it highly reflective (high albedo).

solar-system.question: What is a ‘year’ on a planet?
solar-system.correctAnswer: One full orbit around the Sun
incorrectAnswers: One full spin on its axis
solar-system.explanation: Orbital period defines a planetary year.

stars.question: What powers most stars’ long-term energy output?
stars.explanation: Stars shine by fusing light nuclei under extreme temperature and pressure.

stars.question: What is a main sequence star?
stars.correctAnswer: A star fusing hydrogen into helium in its core (stable phase)
incorrectAnswers: A star that only reflects sunlight
stars.explanation: Sun-like stars spend most of their lives on the main sequence.

stars.question: What does a star’s color roughly indicate?
incorrectAnswers: Mass only with no temperature link
stars.explanation: Hotter surfaces emit more energetic light, shifting color.

stars.question: What is a red giant?
stars.correctAnswer: A late-stage star that expanded after core hydrogen exhaustion
stars.explanation: Low- to intermediate-mass stars swell when evolving off the main sequence.

stars.question: What is a neutron star?
stars.correctAnswer: An extremely dense remnant after a massive star’s core collapse
incorrectAnswers: A normal star like the Sun
stars.explanation: Neutron stars can form from supernova cores under extreme gravity.

stars.question: What is a black hole?
stars.correctAnswer: A region where gravity is so strong that light cannot escape the event horizon
incorrectAnswers: A hole in the galactic disk
An empty bubble of vacuum only
stars.explanation: Black holes are predicted by general relativity with an event horizon.

stars.question: What is parallax used for in astronomy?
stars.explanation: Earth’s orbit provides a baseline for triangulating stellar distances.

stars.question: What is absolute magnitude related to?
stars.correctAnswer: Intrinsic brightness (compared at a standard distance)
incorrectAnswers: How bright a star looks from Earth only
stars.explanation: Apparent magnitude depends on distance; absolute magnitude standardizes comparison.

stars.question: What is a spectroscope useful for with starlight?
stars.correctAnswer: Identifying chemical composition and motion via spectral lines
incorrectAnswers: Measuring a star’s exact size with a ruler
stars.explanation: Spectral lines reveal elements and Doppler shifts.

stars.question: What is the Sun’s general spectral class (common label)?
stars.explanation: The Sun is often called a G2V star.

stars.question: What is a binary star system?
stars.correctAnswer: Two stars orbiting a common center of mass
stars.explanation: Many stars form bound pairs or multiples.

stars.question: Why do stars twinkle as seen from Earth?
stars.correctAnswer: Earth’s atmosphere bends starlight as turbulence changes
stars.explanation: Scintillation is an atmospheric effect for point sources.

galaxies.question: What galaxy contains our solar system?
galaxies.explanation: We orbit within the Milky Way, a barred spiral galaxy.

galaxies.question: What is the approximate shape of the Milky Way?
galaxies.explanation: Observations indicate a bar in the central bulge and spiral arms.

galaxies.question: What is a galaxy mostly made of?
galaxies.correctAnswer: Stars, gas, dust, and dark matter (gravitationally)
galaxies.explanation: Galaxies are gravitationally bound systems with visible and dark components.

galaxies.question: What is a quasar?
galaxies.correctAnswer: An extremely luminous active galactic nucleus
galaxies.explanation: Quasars are powered by accretion onto supermassive black holes.

galaxies.question: What is the Andromeda Galaxy relative to ours?
galaxies.correctAnswer: The nearest large spiral galaxy (on cosmic scales)
galaxies.explanation: Andromeda (M31) is a major neighbor galaxy.

galaxies.question: What is the Local Group?
galaxies.correctAnswer: A small cluster of galaxies including Milky Way and Andromeda
galaxies.explanation: The Local Group is our neighborhood galaxy cluster.

galaxies.question: What does Hubble’s law relate for galaxies?
galaxies.correctAnswer: Recession speed and distance (on large scales)
galaxies.explanation: Greater distance correlates with higher recession velocity in expanding universe.

galaxies.question: What is gravitational lensing?
galaxies.correctAnswer: Mass bends light paths, magnifying or distorting background sources
incorrectAnswers: Lenses made of glass in space
galaxies.explanation: General relativity predicts light deflection by mass.

galaxies.question: What is a galaxy cluster?
galaxies.correctAnswer: Many galaxies bound together by gravity
galaxies.explanation: Clusters are the largest gravitationally bound structures of galaxies.

galaxies.question: What is interstellar dust’s effect on observations?
galaxies.correctAnswer: It can redden and block visible light (extinction)
incorrectAnswers: It creates comet tails on Earth
galaxies.explanation: Dust scatters and absorbs light, changing colors and brightness.

galaxies.question: What is a rotation curve used to infer in galaxies?
galaxies.correctAnswer: How mass is distributed (including dark matter hints)
galaxies.explanation: Flat rotation curves in spirals motivated dark matter models.

galaxies.question: What is cosmic redshift?
galaxies.correctAnswer: Light wavelengths stretch as space expands
galaxies.explanation: Expansion increases observed wavelength for distant sources.

cosmology.question: What does the Big Bang model describe?
cosmology.correctAnswer: The early hot, dense state and expansion of the observable universe
incorrectAnswers: An explosion at a center point in space only
cosmology.explanation: Cosmology models expansion from early conditions; it is not an explosion ‘into’ pre-existing space.

cosmology.question: What is cosmic microwave background radiation?
cosmology.correctAnswer: Remnant glow from when the universe became transparent to light
cosmology.explanation: The CMB is a key observational pillar of hot early universe models.

cosmology.question: What is dark energy often invoked to explain?
cosmology.explanation: Late-time acceleration appears in distance–redshift data.

cosmology.question: What is dark matter inferred from?
cosmology.correctAnswer: Gravitational effects that exceed visible matter alone
cosmology.explanation: Galaxy dynamics and lensing suggest extra unseen mass.

cosmology.question: What is the observable universe?
cosmology.correctAnswer: The region from which light has had time to reach us
cosmology.explanation: Cosmic horizon limits what we can observe.

cosmology.question: What is inflation (cosmology) broadly?
cosmology.correctAnswer: A proposed early rapid expansion episode
cosmology.explanation: Inflation addresses horizon and flatness puzzles in some models.

cosmology.question: What is nucleosynthesis in early universe context?
cosmology.correctAnswer: Formation of light nuclei like hydrogen and helium
incorrectAnswers: Formation of iron in stars only today
cosmology.explanation: Primordial nucleosynthesis matches observed light-element abundances.

cosmology.question: What does ‘redshift z’ measure in cosmology?
cosmology.correctAnswer: How much wavelengths have stretched relative to emission
cosmology.explanation: Higher z corresponds to earlier cosmic times for distant sources.

cosmology.question: What is the cosmological principle (approximation)?
cosmology.correctAnswer: The universe is roughly homogeneous and isotropic on large scales
incorrectAnswers: Galaxies line up in a grid
cosmology.explanation: Models simplify large-scale structure with statistical uniformity.

cosmology.question: What is a light-year?
cosmology.correctAnswer: Distance light travels in one year
cosmology.explanation: It is a distance, not a time.

cosmology.question: What is spacetime in relativity?
cosmology.correctAnswer: A combined model where space and time form a four-dimensional geometry

cosmology.question: Why can distant galaxies appear as they were long ago?
cosmology.correctAnswer: Light travel time means we see earlier epochs
cosmology.explanation: Looking far away is looking back in time.

space-missions.question: What is low Earth orbit (LEO) used for?
space-missions.correctAnswer: Many satellites and crewed stations because it is accessible
incorrectAnswers: Only solar observations outside Earth orbit
space-missions.explanation: LEO is relatively close and requires less energy than deep space.

space-missions.question: Why do rockets need multiple stages?
space-missions.correctAnswer: To shed mass and improve efficiency as fuel is burned
space-missions.explanation: Staging drops empty tanks to accelerate payload better.

space-missions.question: What is a Hohmann transfer (concept)?
space-missions.correctAnswer: An efficient orbital maneuver between two circular orbits
space-missions.explanation: Uses two engine burns to change orbital energy.

space-missions.question: What is a rover mission’s main advantage on Mars?
space-missions.correctAnswer: Mobility to study diverse rocks and terrain
space-missions.explanation: Rovers extend in-situ exploration beyond a single landing site.

space-missions.question: Why are planetary landings challenging?
space-missions.correctAnswer: Thin atmospheres, high speeds, and precise timing
space-missions.explanation: Entry, descent, and landing must dissipate huge kinetic energy.

space-missions.question: What is a space telescope’s advantage over many ground telescopes?
space-missions.correctAnswer: It avoids much atmospheric distortion and absorption
incorrectAnswers: It is always closer to stars
space-missions.explanation: HST and JWST observe above (or beyond) atmospheric limits for many bands.

space-missions.question: What is deep space communication delay caused by?
space-missions.correctAnswer: Finite speed of light over large distances
space-missions.explanation: Mars signals can take many minutes one way.

space-missions.question: What is a gravity assist (flyby)?
space-missions.correctAnswer: Using a planet’s gravity to change a spacecraft’s speed and path

space-missions.question: What is the International Space Station (ISS) mainly for?
space-missions.correctAnswer: Research and technology demonstration in microgravity
space-missions.explanation: ISS supports long-duration human spaceflight science.

space-missions.question: Why do astronauts appear weightless in orbit?
space-missions.correctAnswer: They are in free fall around Earth (microgravity environment)
incorrectAnswers: There is no gravity in space
Earth’s gravity ends at the atmosphere
space-missions.explanation: Orbit is continuous free fall with high tangential speed.

space-missions.question: What is sample return (mission goal)?
space-missions.correctAnswer: Bring physical samples back to Earth for lab analysis
space-missions.explanation: Returned samples enable precise instruments on Earth.

space-missions.question: What is planetary protection?
space-missions.correctAnswer: Policies to limit biological contamination between worlds
space-missions.explanation: Prevents harmful forward/back contamination during exploration.

telescopes.question: What does a refracting telescope use to bend light?
telescopes.explanation: Early telescopes used objective lenses; modern designs vary.

telescopes.question: What do reflecting telescopes primarily use to collect light?
telescopes.explanation: Large mirrors can be supported more easily than huge lenses.

telescopes.question: Why are larger apertures desired?
telescopes.correctAnswer: They collect more light and improve resolution

telescopes.question: What is angular resolution?
telescopes.correctAnswer: Ability to distinguish small separations on the sky

telescopes.question: What is adaptive optics used for?
telescopes.correctAnswer: Correcting atmospheric distortion in real time
telescopes.explanation: Ground telescopes can rival space seeing in some bands with AO.

telescopes.question: Why are many observatories on high mountains?
telescopes.correctAnswer: Thinner, drier air reduces absorption and turbulence

telescopes.question: What is interferometry in astronomy?
telescopes.correctAnswer: Combining signals from separated telescopes to boost resolution
telescopes.explanation: Very long baselines improve angular detail.

telescopes.question: Why observe in radio wavelengths?
telescopes.correctAnswer: Some objects emit strongly in radio (and dust is transparent)
incorrectAnswers: Radio waves don’t exist in space
telescopes.explanation: Radio astronomy reveals cold gas, pulsars, and AGN.

telescopes.question: Why observe in infrared?
telescopes.correctAnswer: To see through dust and study cooler objects
telescopes.explanation: JWST’s infrared capabilities probe star formation and exoplanet atmospheres.

telescopes.question: What is chromatic aberration?
telescopes.correctAnswer: Lens dispersion makes colors focus at different points
telescopes.explanation: Achromatic/apochromatic designs reduce this in refractors.

telescopes.question: What is a CCD/CMOS sensor’s role?
telescopes.correctAnswer: Detect photons and record images electronically
telescopes.explanation: Modern astrophotography relies on digital detectors.

telescopes.question: What is a spectrograph on a telescope used for?
telescopes.correctAnswer: Spreading light into wavelengths to study composition and motion
